name: El Cid's Exile Trail
name_original: Monasterio de San Pedro Cardeña — circular desde Castrillo del Val
description: Hike 8.8 miles (14.1 km) on this moderate circular trail from Castrillo del Val to the historic Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña.
region_intro: Hiking around Castrillo Del Val offers diverse landscapes, situated between the Arlanzón valley and the foothills of the Sierra de la Demanda. The region features a relict forest with oak and holm oak trees, providing a lush environment for exploration. The Arlanzón River is a significant natural feature, with trails running alongside it. Hikers can experience varied topography and scenic views, including wide gullies and low scrub. Best hiking trails around Castrillo Del Val The most popular hiking route is El Cid's Exile Trail, a 8.8 miles (14.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 49 minutes to complete. This moderate path connects Castrillo del Val to the historic Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cueva Carrera – Carrascal Cave loop from Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña, a moderate 6.5 miles (10.5 km) path. This route is suitable for various skill levels and starts from the monastery. Local hikers also love the Carrascal Cave – Cueva Carrera loop from Castrillo del Val, a 4.1 miles (6.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 47 minutes. Hiking around Castrillo Del Val is defined by relict forests, the Arlanzón River valley, and the initial stretches of the Sierra de la Demanda. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging routes. The routes in Castrillo Del Val are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 240 reviews. More than 1,700 hikers have used komoot to explore Castrillo Del Val's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Castrillo Del Val?
A: Castrillo Del Val offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 160 routes recorded on komoot. These trails cater to different fitness levels, including 66 easy, 83 moderate, and 13 difficult options.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Castrillo Del Val?
A: The region around Castrillo Del Val is characterized by diverse landscapes. You'll find lush relict forests dominated by oak and holm oak trees, trails along the significant Arlanzón River, and the initial stretches of the Sierra de la Demanda, offering varied topography and scenic views. Look out for wide gullies with holm oaks and low scrub, and the Pasaderilla stream.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Castrillo Del Val?
A: Yes, Castrillo Del Val has numerous family-friendly trails. Many of the 66 easy routes are suitable for families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10462828" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carrascal Cave – Cueva Carrera loop from Castrillo del Val&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 6.6 km path that can be completed in under two hours, offering comfortable and open paths through the forest.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I explore on the trails?
A: Hiking in Castrillo Del Val allows you to discover several interesting landmarks. The historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/171220" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña&lt;/a&gt; is a focal point for several routes, including the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042302635/el-cid-s-exile-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cid's Exile Trail&lt;/a&gt;. You can also find fascinating prehistoric elements like megalithic rock formations and caves such as the "Cuevas del Carrascal" and "Cueva del Portal de Belén," with the latter being an ancient dwelling. The Peñalada menhir, a large vertical megalith, is another intriguing discovery in the area.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular moderate circular path is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042302635/el-cid-s-exile-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cid's Exile Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which connects Castrillo del Val to the Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38271747" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva Carrera – Carrascal Cave loop from Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Castrillo Del Val?
A: The hiking trails around Castrillo Del Val are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 240 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, from relict forests to river valleys, and the well-maintained paths that offer scenic views of the Arlanzón valley and the Sierra de la Demanda foothills.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Castrillo Del Val?
A: The region's diverse landscapes, including forests and river trails, make it enjoyable across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural colors, ideal for exploring the relict forests and riverbanks. Summer can be warm, but shaded forest paths provide respite. Winter hiking is also possible, though conditions may vary.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: For experienced trekkers seeking a challenge, there are 13 difficult routes available. One such option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40641473" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arlanzón River – Fuente del Prior Beach loop from Castañares&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 27.9 km trail that requires good fitness and sure-footedness.

Q: Can I combine hiking with visits to nearby cultural sites?
A: Absolutely. Castrillo Del Val's location near Burgos and the Sierra de Atapuerca means you can easily combine your hikes with cultural excursions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2729971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Atapuerca Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage site, is a short distance away, offering deep historical context. In Burgos, you can visit the magnificent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/171220" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burgos Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3536790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of Burgos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any trails that follow the Arlanzón River?
A: Yes, the Arlanzón River is a significant natural feature in the region, and several trails run alongside it. These paths offer opportunities to enjoy the riverside scenery and are popular for activities like hiking and sport fishing. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40641473" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arlanzón River – Fuente del Prior Beach loop from Castañares&lt;/a&gt; is one such route that closely follows the river.

Q: What are some unique natural features to look out for on the trails?
A: Beyond the relict forests and the Arlanzón River, hikers can discover unique natural features such as the Vagauada del Arroyo de la Pasaderilla, a wide gully with holm oaks and scrub through which the Pasaderilla stream flows. The area is also known for its impressive caves, including the "Cuevas del Carrascal" and the "Cueva del Portal de Belén," which was an ancient dwelling.
tips: When you come from the town of Castrillo del Val in the direction of Cardeñajimeno, this road, which is higher than the other areas, allows you to see beautiful views of the area of Burgos and its capital.

It preserves parts of the Romanesque style; the church is Gothic and part of the façade dates from the eighteenth century. Since 1942 it has been inhabited by Cistercian monks and produces wines and liqueurs from medicinal herbs.
This monastery received the Cid at the time of his exile, along with his wife and daughters, and they lived there until the end of his life.
A small cave in a place that one does not expect, where you realize that you are in karst terrain and probably the entire subsoil is full of unknown cavities.

faqs.question: Where does the Monasterio de San Pedro Cardeña circular trail start, and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The circular trail typically starts from Castrillo del Val. While specific parking details aren't provided, you can generally find parking within the village or near the trailhead. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this hike?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as moderate. It's suitable for hikers with good physical condition, offering a balanced challenge without being overly strenuous. The route covers approximately 14 km with manageable ascents and descents.

faqs.question: Is the Monasterio de San Pedro Cardeña circular trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself is generally accessible, specific rules for dogs within the Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña or its immediate grounds may vary. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash and ensure they are well-behaved, especially around historical sites and other hikers. Please check monastery visitor guidelines if you plan to enter the premises with your dog.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or to pay an entrance fee to hike the trail itself. Access to the natural areas is generally free. However, if you plan to visit the interior of the Monastery of San Pedro de Cardeña, there may be an entrance fee for tours or specific sections.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed during most seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant hiking conditions with mild temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Summer can be warm, so an early start is recommended, while winter might bring colder weather, though snow is less common in this region.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail features varied terrain, including easily accessible paths and possibly some roads. You'll traverse landscapes with beautiful views of the Burgos area, passing through areas that may include vineyards and tranquil countryside. The monastery itself is nestled in a small valley.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Monasterio de San Pedro Cardeña circular trail?
faqs.answer: The undeniable highlight is the historic Monastery of San Pedro de Cardeña, known for its connection to El Cid. Other notable features include the Path towards Cardeñajimeno and the Cueva Carrera. Outside the monastery, you can also find the monolith marking the traditional burial place of Babieca, El Cid's horse.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with several other paths. You'll find parts of the Sendero del Destierro del Cid, the Camino del Cid (Senderista), and the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, among others, along the way.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or amenities near the trailhead or monastery?
faqs.answer: Castrillo del Val, the starting point, is a village where you might find basic amenities. At the Monastery of San Pedro de Cardeña, the monks are known for producing local products like wine, liqueurs, and cheese, which are often available for purchase. For more extensive options like cafes or restaurants, you might need to visit nearby larger towns.

faqs.question: What historical significance does the Monasterio de San Pedro de Cardeña hold?
faqs.answer: The monastery has a rich history, most notably its profound connection to El Cid Campeador. Legend says El Cid left his family under the monks' protection during his exile, and his remains, along with those of Doña Jimena, rested here for centuries. The monastery is also recognized as an associated element of the Camino de Santiago, a UNESCO World Heritage site.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Monasterio de San Pedro Cardeña from Castrillo del Val is a circular route, meaning you will end your hike at or near your starting point in Castrillo del Val.

waypoints.name: Monastery of San Pedro de Cardeña
